skill.name: create-subagent
skill.description: Guidance for designing, configuring, and testing Claude Code subagents with file-based agents or Task tool dispatch.
skill.summary: Create focused Claude Code subagents with clear roles, tools, prompts, and invocation rules.
skill.author: Cygnusfear

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced many high-severity matches, but inspection shows most are Markdown examples or prose false positives. The real risk is medium because the skill teaches persistent agent configuration, broad tool grants, and a permission bypass mode that users could apply unsafely.

medium_findings.title: Broad Agent Tool Permissions
medium_findings.description: The guide shows agents with Write, Edit, Bash, WebFetch, and WebSearch access, and notes that omitted tools inherit all tools. This is legitimate configuration guidance, but unsafe agents can edit files, run commands, or access the web.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The risky permissions are explicitly documented in agent examples. No executable code uses them directly, so the risk depends on user-created agents.

medium_findings.title: Permission Bypass Mode Is Documented
medium_findings.description: The skill documents bypassPermissions as a mode that skips permission prompts. This can be useful in controlled environments, but it can also remove an important safety gate for agent actions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The permission mode is named and described clearly. The surrounding text does not force its use or hide the risk.

medium_findings.title: Persistent Hidden Agent Configuration
medium_findings.description: The guide instructs users to create project and user agent files under .claude/agents and ~/.claude/agents. Persistent user-level agents can affect future Claude Code sessions if configured carelessly.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The hidden configuration paths and directory creation commands are directly present. They are expected for this skill, but persistence is a real operational risk.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Matches Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The many Ruby or shell backtick findings are Markdown fences, inline tool names, or sample commands. I did not find executable Ruby code or hidden shell execution in the inspected files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The matched locations are inside Markdown examples, not runnable source files. The only shell snippets create documented agent directories.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Matches Are Textual False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm findings occur in prose or Markdown template text. I did not find cryptographic code, hashing calls, or encryption guidance in these locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are descriptions and agent frontmatter examples. No evidence found for actual MD5, SHA1, DES, or other weak crypto usage.

low_findings.title: Network Reconnaissance Matches Are Textual False Positives
low_findings.description: The network reconnaissance findings point to test-writing and security-review prose, not scanning commands or network tooling. No evidence found for reconnaissance behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines contain natural-language instructions such as writing tests and assuming source access. They do not invoke network scanners or collect targets.

dangerous_patterns.title: Broad Agent Tool Permissions
dangerous_patterns.description: The guide shows agents with Write, Edit, Bash, WebFetch, and WebSearch access, and notes that omitted tools inherit all tools. This is legitimate configuration guidance, but unsafe agents can edit files, run commands, or access the web.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The risky permissions are explicitly documented in agent examples. No executable code uses them directly, so the risk depends on user-created agents.

dangerous_patterns.title: Permission Bypass Mode Is Documented
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents bypassPermissions as a mode that skips permission prompts. This can be useful in controlled environments, but it can also remove an important safety gate for agent actions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The permission mode is named and described clearly. The surrounding text does not force its use or hide the risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Persistent Hidden Agent Configuration
dangerous_patterns.description: The guide instructs users to create project and user agent files under .claude/agents and ~/.claude/agents. Persistent user-level agents can affect future Claude Code sessions if configured carelessly.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The hidden configuration paths and directory creation commands are directly present. They are expected for this skill, but persistence is a real operational risk.

content.value_statement: Reusable subagents are hard to design when roles, tools, and permissions are unclear. This skill gives structured guidance for creating focused Claude Code agents safely.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to use file-based subagents or Task tool dispatch.
Defines required and optional agent frontmatter fields.
Provides templates for reviewers, debuggers, researchers, and writers.
Shows tool and permission choices for different agent responsibilities.
Gives testing and iteration steps for improving agent behavior.
limitations: It provides guidance only and does not create files automatically.
It assumes familiarity with Claude Code agent concepts.
Unsafe permission choices can still create risky agents.
Examples may require updates if Claude Code agent syntax changes.

use_cases.title: Build a Review Agent
use_cases.description: Create a focused code review subagent with read-only tools and clear severity reporting.

use_cases.title: Delegate Research Work
use_cases.description: Design a research subagent that explores a codebase and reports evidence without filling the main context.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team Agents
use_cases.description: Create project-level agent templates that teams can review, version, and reuse across workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Simple Agent
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for one focused role.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me create a Claude Code subagent for code review. Use read-only tools and explain each frontmatter field.

prompt_templates.title: Choose Agent Permissions
prompt_templates.scenario: Compare safe tool choices for a specific task.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a debugging subagent for this project. Recommend the minimum tools it needs and explain permission risks.

prompt_templates.title: Refine an Existing Agent
prompt_templates.scenario: Improve reliability after observing weak behavior.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this subagent prompt and make it more reliable. Add constraints, workflow steps, and output expectations.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Multi-Agent Workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced delegation with parallel agents and synthesis.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a multi-agent workflow for auditing authentication, authorization, and session handling in parallel, then synthesizing findings.

output_examples.input: Create a read-only security reviewer agent.
output_examples.output: A named security review agent with read-only tools, threat-modeling steps, severity rules, and concise report formatting.
A sharper role definition, specific trigger conditions, narrower tools, clear constraints, and examples of expected behavior.

output_examples.input: Plan parallel agents for a large refactor review.
output_examples.output: A delegation plan that splits independent review areas, defines each agent mission, and adds a synthesis step.

best_practices: Grant the smallest tool set that lets the agent complete its task.
Use project-level agents when the team should review and version changes.
Test agents with representative tasks before relying on automatic invocation.
anti_patterns: Do not give every agent Bash or web access by default.
Do not use permission bypass modes for unreviewed or broad agent prompts.
Do not create vague helper agents without clear triggers and constraints.

faq.question: Does this skill create subagent files automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance, templates, and examples for creating Claude Code subagents.

faq.question: Can it help with Task tool dispatch?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains dynamic Task tool invocation and when to use it instead of persistent agent files.

faq.question: Is this safe for shared projects?
faq.answer: It can be safe when agents use limited tools and project-level files are reviewed before use.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill documents broad tools, hidden agent directories, and permission bypass options that require careful use.

faq.question: Does it support Claude Code only?
faq.answer: The content focuses on Claude Code subagents, but the design principles can inform Codex and Claude workflows.

faq.question: What should I avoid first?
faq.answer: Avoid broad tools, automatic invocation, and permission bypass settings until the agent prompt is reviewed and tested.
